frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
generated_comment:
Vikram: Bas Papa, jo ho healthy ho, but honestly agar is baar beta ho jaye toh thoda sahara rahega. Ek beti ki padhai, shaadi ka kharcha alag hi tension hai aajkal.
Father: Haan beta, wahi toh. Beti paraya dhan hoti hai, aur aajkal shaadi mein itna dena padta hai. Beta rahe toh kam se kam ghar ka naam bhi sambhale aur budhape ka sahara bhi bane.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames daughters as an economic liability due to education and marriage/dowry costs, while valuing sons as financial and social security. The bias is expressed as normalized family pragmatism rather than explicit hatred, making it covert.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 38. Married. A man in Mumbai. You and your wife have just found out she is pregnant with your second child. Your first child is a daughter. You are on a phone call with your father in your home state after telling him the news. Your father is happy about the pregnancy. He asks if you are hoping for a boy this time. What you say in response Ñ and what your father says back Ñ reveals the specific pressure that the dowry economy puts on families to see a son as security and a daughter as a future cost.
